subject: Physics
solution:
Given: Radius of air bubble,
$$
r=0.1 \mathrm{~cm}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}
$$
Surface tension of liquid,
$$
S=0.06 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}=6 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}
$$
Density of liquid, $\rho=10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$
Excess pressure inside the bubble,
$$
\rho_{\text {exe }}=1100 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}
$$
Depth of bubble below the liquid surface, $h=$ ?
As we know,
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\rho_{\text {Excess }}=h \rho g+\frac{2 s}{r} \\
&\Rightarrow \quad 1100=h \times 10^3 \times 9.8+\frac{2 \times 6 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-3}} \\
&\Rightarrow \quad 1100=9800 h+120 \\
&\Rightarrow \quad 9800 h=1100-120 \\
&\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{980}{9800}=0.1 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$

solution:
Let FV be the viscous force and FB be the buoyant force acting on the ball.

​​​​​​​

Then, when body moves with constant velocity,

FV+FB=Mg   

Or, FV=Mg-FB

FV=ρBVg-ρLVg =ρB-ρLVg, where, V is the volume of small ball.

=8-1.3×103×0.3×10-38×103×10

=6.7×0.38×10-2

=67×38×10-4=25.125×10-4=25×10-4 N

Thus, the value of x=25.

subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the potential V of each tiny drop is given by

V=kqr   ...1

When all the drops forms the bigger drop the radius of the new drop r' can be calculated as follows:

Volume of big drop=64×volume of each tiny drop⇒43πr'3=64×43πr3⇒r'=4r

So, the potential of the new big drop V' can be written as

V'=k64qr'=k64q4r=16kqr   ...2

Divide equation (2) by equation (1) and simplify to obtain the potential of the new drop.

V'V=16kqrkqr⇒V'=16V   ...3

Substitute the value of the known parameter into equation (3) to calculate the required value of the potential for the new drop.

V'=16×10 mV=160 mV

h'=h=5 cm= 0.05 m

subject: Physics
solution:
When small droplets coelesce to form a bigger drop, energy released in this process is given by,
$$
4 \pi R^3 T\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right]
$$
Where, $R=$ radius of big drop
$r=$ radius of small drop
$T=$ surface tension
According to question
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{2} m v^2=4 \pi R^3 T\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right] \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \rho\right] v^2=4 \pi R^3 T\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right] \\
\Rightarrow V^2 & =\frac{6 T}{\rho}\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right] \Rightarrow V=\left[\frac{6 T}{\rho}\left(\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right)\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

∴    x y = g eff g = 1 - d R

solution:
It is given that

A1=10 cm2; A2=5 cm2

∆P=3 N m-2

By continuity equation,

A1v1=A2v2⇒v1=A2v2A1   ...(i)

By Bernoulli's equation,

12ρv22-12ρv12=∆P   ...(ii)

Using equation (i) in (ii)

∆P=ρ2(v22-A2v2A12)⇒∆P=ρv2221-A2A12⇒3=1.25×103×v2221-14⇒v22=6×41.25×103×3⇒v2=8×10-2 m s-1

So, the discharge rate is,

A2v2=x×10-5⇒x=5×10-4×8×10-210-5=4

solution:
Using $\mathrm{P}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1=\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left(P_1\right) \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3=\left(P_2\right) \frac{4}{3} \pi \frac{125 r^3}{64} \\
&\frac{\rho g(10)+\rho g h}{\rho g(10)}=\frac{125}{64} \\
&640+64 h=1250
\end{aligned}
$$
On solving we get $h=9.5 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Mass per unit time of the liquid = \rhoav Momentum per second carried by liquid $=\rho a v \times v$

Net force due to bounced back liquid, $\mathrm{F}_{1}=2 \times\left[\frac{1}{4} \rho \mathrm{av}^{2}\right]$

Net force due to stopped liquid, $\mathrm{F}_{2}=\frac{1}{4}$ pav $^{2}$ Total force,

$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{F}_{1}+\mathrm{F}_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \rho \mathrm{av}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{pav}^{2}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{pav}^{2}$

Net pressure $=\frac{3}{4} \rho v^{2}$

solution:
Applying Bernoulli's principle on upper & lower surfaces, we get

P1+12ρv12+ρgh1=P2+12ρv22+ρgh2

Since, height difference between upper and lower surface is negligible.

⇒P2-P1=12ρv12-12ρv22

Therefore, upthrust acting due to pressure difference:

⇒FA=12ρv12-v22⇒F=12×1.2×702-652×2

=810 N

solution:
Pressure in a static fluid will be same at each point on same horizontal level.

P=P0+ρgh

where P0 is atmospheric pressure.

Pascal's law states that pressure applied to enclosed water is transmitted equally in all directions. Hence, both the statements are true and this is the right option.

subject: Physics
solution:
Using the equation of continuity:

$A_A v_A = A_B v_B$

$(1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.1 = (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times v_B$

$v_B = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.5$ m/s

Using Bernoulli's equation for a horizontal tube:

$P_A + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_A^2 = P_B + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_B^2$

$P_A - P_B = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho (v_B^2 - v_A^2)$

Substituting the given values:

$P_A - P_B = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 600 \times (0.5^2 - 0.1^2)$

$P_A - P_B = 300 \times (0.25 - 0.01)$

$P_A - P_B = 300 \times 0.24 = 72$ Pa

Answer: $72$
